narrative:
Dear [Student's Name],

As a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength and determination it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, an LSAT score of 36, upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and school decile rankings of 3rd in Year 1 and 1st in Year 3 based on your grades. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success using past student data, estimated a 73.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam— a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass on their first try.

The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1st, which stands well below the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. This outweighed the positive pull from your LSAT score of 36, which is close to the dataset average of 36.7 and helped nudge the odds slightly toward passing. Your Year 1 decile of 3rd, also below the average of 5.71, added further concern by suggesting inconsistent early grades relative to others in the dataset.

Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80—lower than the typical 3.21—contributed modestly toward the failure prediction, while your upper-middle family income and full-time status offered small boosts in the opposite direction, aligning with patterns of students who tend to succeed. Overall, your academic deciles painted a picture riskier than most applicants', tipping the scales despite your solid LSAT.

While this outcome stings, especially given the barriers many Black women face in higher education, it's not the end of your path—many have rebounded stronger. Consider retaking courses or the LSAT to boost your deciles and scores, seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students, or applying to programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You're capable of rewriting this story; keep pushing forward with the resilience that got you here.
